div.head: Mr. Plumb to Mr. Seward.
div.p: Sir: I have the honor to transmit herewith translation of a decree issued by this government on the 21st instant, closing the port of Mazatlan to foreign and coasting trade, so long as it is withdrawn from obedience to the government.
I am, very respectfully, your obedient servant,

postscript.p: Hon. William H. Seward, Secretary of State, Washington, D. C.

frus:attachment.head: Decree closing the Port of Mazatlan.

opener.dateline: [From the Diario Oficial, City of Mexico, April 22, 1868.—Translation.]

frus:attachment.p: Department of Treasury and Public Credit.–Section 1.
Under this date the citizen President of the republic has been pleased to address to me the following decree:
Benito Juarez, constitutional President of the United Mexican States, to the inhabitants of the same, be it known:
That, in use of the faculty conceded to me by section 14 of article 85 of the federal constitution, I have thought proper to decree the following:
Sole Article: The port of Mazatlan is declared closed to the foreign and coasting trade so long as it is withdrawn from obedience to the government.
Wherefore I order that this be printed, published, circulated, and due compliance given to it.

closer.dateline: Dated at the palace of the federal government at Mexico, the 21st of April, 1868.

postscript.p: The Citizen Matias Romero, Minister of Treasury and Public Credit.
And I communicate the same to you for your knowledge and the consequent effects.
